questions.title: Lead Status Transition Rules
questions.description: The OpenAPI spec defines 7 lead statuses (NEW, CONTACTED, TRIAL_SCHEDULED, TRIAL_ATTENDED, ENROLLED, DROPPED, NOT_INTERESTED) but no explicit transition matrix exists. The updateLeadStatus endpoint has a business_rule_guard inferred from GP-04, but valid transitions are not defined. Which transitions should be allowed?
questions.impact: Determines whether admin can freely move leads between statuses or must follow a strict workflow. Affects the business_rule_guard implementation on PATCH /api/leads/{leadId}/status.

options.label: Forward-only with terminal states
options.resolutionEffect: Enforce ordered transitions: NEW -> CONTACTED -> TRIAL_SCHEDULED -> TRIAL_ATTENDED -> ENROLLED. DROPPED and NOT_INTERESTED reachable from any non-terminal state. No backward transitions. Admin cannot reactivate DROPPED/NOT_INTERESTED leads.
cons: Admin loses flexibility for edge cases
Cannot undo mistakes without DB intervention

options.label: Forward-only with admin override
options.resolutionEffect: Default forward path: NEW -> CONTACTED -> TRIAL_SCHEDULED -> TRIAL_ATTENDED -> ENROLLED. DROPPED and NOT_INTERESTED reachable from any state. Admin CAN move backward (e.g., DROPPED -> CONTACTED) as an explicit override. All transitions logged in LeadActivity.
pros: Maintains workflow discipline while allowing corrections
Handles real-world edge cases (lead returns after dropping)
cons: Slightly more complex business rule guard
Analytics must account for non-linear paths

options.label: Fully open transitions
options.resolutionEffect: Any status can transition to any other status. No validation beyond checking the new status is a valid enum value. Relies on admin discipline.

questions.selectionRationale: Forward-only with admin override provides workflow discipline while handling real-world edge cases. Auto-advance from GP-04 follows the forward path; manual admin changes are logged as overrides in LeadActivity.

questions.title: Enrollment Status Transition Rules
questions.description: Enrollment has 3 statuses (ACTIVE, CANCELLED, COMPLETED) but no explicit transition rules. The BE policy infers ACTIVE->CANCELLED requires cancellationReason. Can CANCELLED enrollments be reactivated? Can COMPLETED go back to ACTIVE?
questions.impact: Determines enrollment lifecycle rigidity. Affects updateEnrollmentStatus business_rule_guard and whether cancelled enrollments can be recovered without creating new ones.

options.label: Strict one-way transitions only
options.resolutionEffect: ACTIVE -> CANCELLED (requires cancellationReason). ACTIVE -> COMPLETED. No reverse transitions. CANCELLED and COMPLETED are terminal states. To re-enroll, parent must create a new enrollment.
pros: Payment tracking stays clean (one payment per enrollment)

options.label: Allow reactivation from CANCELLED
options.resolutionEffect: ACTIVE -> CANCELLED, ACTIVE -> COMPLETED, CANCELLED -> ACTIVE. COMPLETED is terminal. Reactivation clears cancellationReason and restores the enrollment. Payment record must be re-evaluated.
cons: Payment state becomes complex (was it refunded? re-issued?)
Capacity must be re-checked on reactivation

options.label: Fully flexible transitions
options.resolutionEffect: Any transition between ACTIVE, CANCELLED, COMPLETED is allowed. Admin has full control.

questions.selectionRationale: Strict one-way transitions keep the MVP simple. Payment is 1:1 with enrollment, and reactivation would complicate that. If an admin cancels by mistake, they can create a new enrollment. COMPLETED is set when the program ends.

questions.title: Enrollment Duplicate Check Scope (Per-Child vs Per-Parent)
questions.description: bookTrial checks duplicates using parentEmail + childName + programId (per child per program), but submitEnrollment checks using parentEmail + programId (per parent per program). GP-01 states 'one enrollment per child per program'. If a parent has two children, they should be able to enroll both in the same program, but the current submitEnrollment logic would block the second enrollment.
questions.impact: Critical for families with multiple children. Inconsistency between trial booking and enrollment creates confusing behavior where a parent can book trials for two children but only enroll one.

options.label: Per child per program (align with bookTrial)
options.resolutionEffect: Change submitEnrollment duplicate check to parentEmail + childName + programId. A parent can enroll multiple children in the same program. Consistent with bookTrial behavior and GP-01 intent.
cons: childName matching is case/whitespace sensitive — needs normalization

options.label: Per parent per program (keep current submitEnrollment)
options.resolutionEffect: Keep submitEnrollment checking parentEmail + programId. One enrollment per parent per program. If a parent has multiple children, they must use different email addresses.
cons: Blocks multi-child families from same program

questions.selectionRationale: GP-01 clearly intends per-child-per-program uniqueness. Aligning submitEnrollment with bookTrial ensures consistency. childName normalization (trim + lowercase compare) mitigates matching issues.

questions.title: Capacity Race Condition Strategy
questions.description: When two parents submit enrollment at the same time for the last spot in a program, concurrent writes could exceed capacity. The submitEnrollment and bookTrial transactions need a concurrency strategy to prevent overbooking.
questions.impact: Without protection, the system can exceed program capacity. Affects data integrity for the most critical public-facing transaction.

options.label: Pessimistic locking (SELECT FOR UPDATE)
options.resolutionEffect: Use Prisma interactive transactions with SELECT FOR UPDATE on the Program row during enrollment/trial booking. Locks the row until the transaction completes, preventing concurrent reads of stale capacity.
cons: Brief lock contention under high concurrency

options.label: Optimistic locking (version check)
options.resolutionEffect: Add a version/updatedAt check to the enrollment count query. If the count changed between read and write, retry the transaction. No row-level locks.

questions.selectionRationale: Single-tenant MVP with low concurrency makes pessimistic locking the pragmatic choice. SELECT FOR UPDATE is straightforward with Prisma interactive transactions. Lock duration is minimal (single transaction).

questions.title: Payment Overdue Threshold Storage Location
questions.description: The payment overdue reminder (HO-04) needs a configurable threshold (default 3 days) to determine when PENDING payments trigger reminder emails. The storage location is unspecified — should it live in LocationSettings (admin-editable via Settings UI) or in environment config (.env)?
questions.impact: Determines whether the admin can change the overdue threshold via the UI or if a developer must update environment variables and redeploy.

options.label: Store in LocationSettings (admin-configurable)
options.resolutionEffect: Add paymentOverdueDays (Int, default 3) field to LocationSettings model. Admin can change it via the Settings screen. The payment_overdue_check cron job reads this value at runtime.
pros: Can differ per location in future multi-location expansion
cons: Settings UI needs a new field

options.label: Store in environment config (.env)
options.resolutionEffect: Add PAYMENT_OVERDUE_DAYS env variable (default 3). Requires redeploy to change. The payment_overdue_check cron job reads from process.env.

questions.selectionRationale: PT-A-01 explicitly states admin-configurable. LocationSettings is the natural home. Adding one Int field is minimal effort.

questions.title: Transactional Email Provider Selection
questions.description: 5 modules require transactional email (auth, leads, enrollment, payments, communication) with 6 automation triggers, delivery tracking, and retry. No provider is specified in any source artifact. This must be resolved before implementation.
questions.impact: Blocks implementation of password reset, trial/enrollment confirmations, payment reminders, and all 6 automation triggers. Affects 5 modules.

options.label: Resend
options.resolutionEffect: Use Resend as the email provider behind an EmailService adapter interface. Simple REST API, excellent developer experience, built for transactional email. Free tier: 100 emails/day.

options.label: SendGrid
options.resolutionEffect: Use SendGrid as the email provider. Most widely documented, battle-tested at scale. Free tier: 100 emails/day.

options.label: AWS SES
options.resolutionEffect: Use AWS SES if deploying on AWS. Cheapest at scale ($0.10/1000 emails). Requires AWS account and domain verification.
pros: No vendor lock-in if already on AWS
cons: Raw API — needs more wrapper code

options.label: Postmark
options.resolutionEffect: Use Postmark for highest deliverability. Focused exclusively on transactional email. Free tier: 100 emails/month.
cons: No marketing email support if needed later

questions.selectionRationale: Resend offers the best DX for a TypeScript/Next.js project, simple API, and sufficient free tier for MVP. Implementation uses an adapter interface so the provider can be swapped later without code changes.

questions.title: Bot Protection for Public Write Endpoints
questions.description: Public write endpoints (bookTrial, submitEnrollment) have no CAPTCHA or bot-protection. Only rate limiting (5 req/15min per IP) is applied. Should CAPTCHA be added for MVP?
questions.impact: Without CAPTCHA, automated bots could submit fake trial bookings or enrollments up to the rate limit. Each submission creates Lead, Trial/Enrollment, Payment, and LeadActivity records.

options.label: Rate limiting only (defer CAPTCHA to post-MVP)
options.resolutionEffect: Keep current rate limiting (5 req/15min per IP) as sole bot protection. Monitor abuse in production. Add CAPTCHA if abuse is detected.
pros: Single-tenant app has low attack surface

options.label: Add invisible reCAPTCHA v3 for MVP
options.resolutionEffect: Add Google reCAPTCHA v3 (invisible, score-based) to bookTrial and submitEnrollment forms. Reject submissions with score < 0.5. No visible CAPTCHA challenge for legitimate users.

questions.selectionRationale: For a single-tenant MVP with low traffic, rate limiting is sufficient. The app creates leads (not financial transactions) so the blast radius of bot submissions is manageable. CAPTCHA adds UX friction and integration complexity that isn't justified yet.

questions.title: Authentication Rate Limit Values
questions.description: Rate limit values for auth endpoints were inferred as defaults (5 req/15min for login/reset, 3 req/15min for forgot-password) since no explicit config exists in the OpenAPI spec.
questions.impact: Affects brute-force protection on login, password reset, and forgot-password endpoints. Too strict = legitimate users locked out. Too loose = vulnerable to credential attacks.

options.label: Accept inferred defaults
options.resolutionEffect: Login: 5 req/15min (ip+email). Forgot-password: 3 req/15min (ip). Reset-password: 5 req/15min (ip). These are standard values for admin-facing auth endpoints.
cons: May be too strict for admins who mistype passwords frequently

options.label: More lenient limits
options.resolutionEffect: Login: 10 req/15min. Forgot-password: 5 req/15min. Reset-password: 10 req/15min.
pros: Less likely to lock out legitimate admins

questions.selectionRationale: Inferred defaults are industry-standard for admin auth. Single-admin app means lockout risk is low (one user, known email). The ip+email scope on login prevents one mistyped password from affecting other users.

questions.title: Lead-Child Relationship Model
questions.description: Lead has @@unique([email]) making it one-per-parent, but also has optional childName/childAge fields. A parent with multiple children gets one Lead record. The Lead's child fields represent whichever child triggered lead creation first. Subsequent children are only tracked in Trial/Enrollment records, not on the Lead.
questions.impact: Affects how the admin sees lead data. If a parent enrolls child A then child B, the Lead still shows child A's name/age. The admin must look at Enrollments to see all children.

options.label: Lead represents the parent (child fields are informational)
options.resolutionEffect: Keep current design. Lead.childName/childAge store the first child mentioned. They are informational context, not authoritative. Child data is authoritative on Trial/Enrollment records. Lead detail screen shows linked trials/enrollments for full child visibility.
pros: Child data preserved on per-booking/enrollment records
Admin sees all children via lead detail relations
cons: Lead's childName/childAge may be stale or misleading

options.label: Remove child fields from Lead
options.resolutionEffect: Remove childName and childAge from the Lead model. Child data only exists on Trial and Enrollment. Lead is purely a parent contact record.
pros: No ambiguity about whose child data is shown
cons: Loses initial child context on lead creation
Auto-created leads from bookTrial lose child info at lead level

questions.selectionRationale: Current design works for MVP. Lead is a parent contact record with optional child context. The lead detail screen already shows linked trials/enrollments which contain authoritative child data per booking.

questions.title: Cascade Delete Behavior for Unspecified Relations
questions.description: Only 2 of 15+ relations have explicit cascade behavior (Lead -> LeadNote/LeadActivity = Cascade, CalendarDate -> Trial = Restrict). The remaining relations (Admin -> Session, Program -> Trial/Enrollment/CalendarDate, LocationSettings -> Lead/Program, Enrollment -> Payment, Trial -> Enrollment) have no specified cascade behavior.
questions.impact: Without explicit cascade rules, Prisma defaults to 'Required' relations throwing errors on parent delete, and 'Optional' relations setting FK to null. This must be explicitly configured to prevent orphaned records or accidental data loss.

options.label: Restrict by default, Cascade only for session/token cleanup
options.resolutionEffect: Default: Restrict (prevent deletion if children exist). Exceptions: Admin -> Session/PasswordResetToken = Cascade (cleanup on admin delete). All other relations use Restrict to prevent accidental data loss. Programs/Locations cannot be deleted if they have dependent records.
pros: Safest default — prevents accidental data loss
Forces proper lifecycle management (archive/close instead of delete)

options.label: Cascade for owned relations, Restrict for shared
options.resolutionEffect: Cascade: Admin -> Session/PasswordResetToken, Lead -> LeadNote/LeadActivity/Trial/Enrollment, Enrollment -> Payment. Restrict: Program -> *, LocationSettings -> *, CalendarDate -> Trial.
pros: Deleting a lead cleans up all related data
cons: Deleting a lead cascades to enrollments and payments — dangerous
Data loss risk for billing-critical records

questions.selectionRationale: Restrict-by-default is the safest approach for an MVP with billing data. Programs use CLOSED status instead of deletion. Leads use DROPPED/NOT_INTERESTED status. Only session/token cleanup cascades (no business value in orphaned sessions).

questions.title: Session Cleanup Strategy
questions.description: Expired sessions accumulate in the database. No cleanup mechanism is specified. Options are a scheduled cron job (periodic batch delete) or lazy deletion (check and delete on each session read).
questions.impact: Low — expired sessions are small records and accumulation is slow in a single-admin app. But cleanup prevents unbounded table growth over time.

options.label: Scheduled cron job (daily cleanup)
options.resolutionEffect: Add a daily cron job that deletes all sessions where expiresAt < now(). Runs during low-traffic hours. Can share the same job scheduler as payment_overdue_check and communication triggers.

options.label: Lazy deletion on session read
options.resolutionEffect: On every getSession call, check if session is expired. If yes, delete it and return 401. Other expired sessions remain until they are accessed.
cons: Adds delete operation to every auth check

questions.selectionRationale: Cron job is cleaner and reuses the job scheduler already needed for payment_overdue_check and communication triggers (TRIAL_APPROACHING, CLASS_START_APPROACHING). Single daily run handles all expired sessions.

questions.title: Facebook/Google Lead Ingestion Method
questions.description: LeadSource enum includes FACEBOOK and GOOGLE values, but no ingestion mechanism is specified. Leads are currently only created via enrollment/trial flows (GP-02) or manual admin entry is not available (no POST /api/leads endpoint).
questions.impact: Low for MVP. Determines how external leads enter the system in future phases.

options.label: Manual only for MVP
options.resolutionEffect: FACEBOOK and GOOGLE source values exist in the enum but are only used when leads are manually attributed. No automated ingestion. A future POST /api/leads endpoint or webhook can be added post-MVP.
pros: Enum values ready for future use
Focus MVP on core enrollment flow
cons: No automated lead capture from ads
Admin must manually enter external leads

options.label: Webhook endpoint for future integration
options.resolutionEffect: Add a POST /api/webhooks/leads endpoint (not in MVP scope but designed). External systems can POST lead data. Validates payload, creates Lead + LEAD_CREATED activity.

questions.selectionRationale: Manual attribution is sufficient for MVP. The enum values are already in place. Webhook/polling integration is a post-MVP feature that can be designed when the specific ad platform requirements are known.

questions.title: Coupon Model Missing updatedAt Field
questions.description: The Coupon model has createdAt but no updatedAt field, yet an updateCoupon endpoint exists (PUT /api/coupons/{couponId}). The global_db_patterns file also flags this inconsistency. Without updatedAt, there's no way to track when a coupon was last modified.
questions.impact: Low — functional impact is minimal, but inconsistent with the timestamp pattern used by all other mutable models. Could cause confusion during debugging or auditing.

options.label: Add updatedAt to Coupon model
options.resolutionEffect: Add updatedAt DateTime @updatedAt to the Coupon model. Consistent with all other mutable models (Admin, Lead, Program, CalendarDate, Template, LocationSettings, Payment).
pros: Consistent with all other mutable models

options.label: Keep Coupon without updatedAt
options.resolutionEffect: Accept the inconsistency. Coupon updates are tracked via audit_log middleware instead of a model field.
cons: No direct way to query 'recently modified coupons'

questions.selectionRationale: Adding @updatedAt is trivial and makes the Coupon model consistent with all other mutable models in the system. Prisma handles it automatically.
